Episode 843: The New Force Driving Business Success

January 17, 2024 by Ivan Misner 6 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

We live in an age of sweeping conflict, widespread skepticism, and intense anxiety. The solution to today’s challenges lies in the 3rd Paradigm. The 1st Paradigm is competition. The 2nd Paradigm is cooperation. The 3rd Paradigm is co-creation.

In the age of co-creation, consumers want to work together with their favorite brands to ensure that products and services are adapted to meet their needs. In addition, many want to make sure that there’s a social cause related to the brand. BNI has been a leader in co-creation since 1986, and it has a social cause: children and education through the BNI Foundation.

Links

  • Get your copy of The 3rd Paradigm: A Radical Shift to Greater Success.
  • Listen to Episode 835: A Radical Shift to Greater Success.
  • Visit the BNI Foundation website.

Complete Transcript of Episode 843

Priscilla:
Hello, everybody, and welcome back to The Official BNI Podcast. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from Live Oak recording studio in Berkeley, California, and I’m joined on the phone today by the Founder and the Chief Visionary Officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan. How are you and where are you?

Ivan:
I am in New Orleans this week for the Transformational Leadership Council (TLC), which was started by Jack Canfield. And of course, I’m going to meet with some local BNI people while I’m here.

Priscilla: 
Oh, boy, that’s such a fun city.

Ivan:
It is a fun city and a great place to buy art. I have bought most of my fine art in New Orleans.

Priscilla:
Oh, interesting. Okay, so what do you have for us today?

Ivan:
Well, I want to talk a little bit more about the book that recently came out, “The 3rd Paradigm,” the book I co-wrote. And I’m going to do several sessions on “The 3rd Paradigm” over the next few weeks. So the latest book, “The 3rd Paradigm,” discusses how businesses should harness the power of consumers who want to have a say in creating and improving the products and services that they buy. And so I want to expand on this concept here in this podcast.

Imagine a world where you can tackle the greatest, most challenging problems with a proven approach that leads to unmatched success. That’s the premise and the promise of the book that I co-wrote with Dr. Heidi Giusto and Dawa Phillips, who’s also a TLC member; I’m seeing him this week. You know, Priscilla, we live in an age of sweeping conflict, widespread skepticism, and intense anxiety. Contention feels pervasive. A balanced discourse is like a thing of the past, and pundits constantly tell us what’s wrong with society. I know I’ve said this in podcasts before, but people complain like it’s an Olympic event — and I checked this, it’s not.

Priscilla: (chuckling)

Ivan:
And we obsess over the massive problems that we see in the world. Negativity seems to be the norm. However, I really believe there’s hope. There’s an answer, and it starts with focusing on the solutions. When people focus on problems, they become world-class experts on “the problem.” But when they focus on solutions, they can become world-class experts on “the solution.” And we believe “the solution,” in part, to today’s massive challenges lies within the 3rd Paradigm.

Now as a reference point, a paradigm is a philosophical framework or discipline within which theories and laws are formulated. And we believe we’re entering the era of the 3rd Paradigm. And so let’s take you on a short journey through what we define as the three paradigms. Now I talked about these three paradigms briefly in episode number 835. I’m going to go into more detail here.

The 1st Paradigm is the era of competition. This paradigm was formulated within the framework of the laws of production by early pioneers of business thinking. Formal theories of management from the 1800s, like Frederick Taylor, the Gilbreths, Max Weber, and others who focused on issues like workflow, economic efficiency, labor productivity. This was the era of “scientific management,” which, interestingly enough, is the beginning of business theory; there really wasn’t much written about business the scientific management. That’s the 1st Paradigm.

The 1st Paradigm was so focused on productivity and competition that it failed to account for the needs of people in the workforce. Competition was about gaining something by defeating your rivals or establishing superiority over them. And it meant having a winner or a loser. I mean, can you imagine living in the production-oriented, competitive model today? Sixty-hour workweeks would be the norm, there would be few–if any–breaks, you’d be expected to work during much of your lunchtime. Forget about safety regulations. Children might be working. You know, in most “developed” nations, that now seems inconceivable, but in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, it was common throughout the world.

So the 2nd Paradigm is the era of cooperation. The paradigm evolved over time as people learned that a strictly production-oriented approach didn’t take into account the interaction of the people involved in the process. This era was about two or more people working together towards shared goals. And the focus on acting together for a common purpose started to evolve in the early 1960s. Around this time, MIT management professor Douglas McGregor published his groundbreaking book, “The Human Side of Enterprise” in 1960, which introduced the concepts of Theory X and Theory Y. And this is stuff I teach in my management… when I taught management theory at university, these are the kind of things that I talked about.

Now, McGregor’s framework highlights the motivating role of job satisfaction, and argues that people can do their work without constant direct supervision. And he insisted that people are one of the most valuable assets driving organizational success. And I know that’s like “obvious” now. But in the 1960s, this was new age; this was crazy that people were an important part of productivity in a business. So we may be puzzled by this because the idea does seem obvious to us, but they weren’t obvious at the start of the 2nd Paradigm. This was, in fact, revolutionary management thinking in its day.

Now, during the time of the 2nd Paradigm, management consultant Peter Drucker developed the MBO process (Management by Objective), where managers and employees could identify common goals and define their areas of responsibility. This is all part of cooperation. Today’s organizations are much more cooperative. Old command-and-control methods have largely been replaced by much more openness and transparency than what we had in the 1st Paradigm. So while the cooperative model, or collaborative model, was a massive improvement from the strictly competitive, production-oriented model, it was still lacking the full potential of the human experience.

And so in the 1980s, John Naisbitt wrote in his groundbreaking book, Megatrends, that humanity is moving towards a “high-tech, high-touch” society, saying that the more technologically advanced we became, the more important was to become highly connected to people. Now, what a lot of people don’t know is, I read this book in the early 1980s. This was the genesis of my thinking about BNI and how putting together a group of people to work together for a common goal of increasing referrals in a structured process. In part, the seed of that came from having read “Megatrends” and John Naisbitt, and it’s one of the reasons why I was able to get John to contribute to a book that I did called “Masters of Networking,” because he really was the person that had me start thinking about the power of getting people together.

And this was several years before I started BNI, getting people together to network effectively in a relationship, in a professional relationship.

Now, it’s really important to note that this transition from one paradigm to another doesn’t happen overnight. It emerges by evolving over time.

Ivan:
So let’s go to the 3rd Paradigm, which is what the book is about. The 3rd Paradigm is the era of co-creation. Now this concept begins where cooperation leaves off. The difference between cooperation and co-creation is the difference between working together and creating together. You may collaborate on a project, but you co-create products and services. Co-creation is a significant step beyond cooperation. It’s about bringing different parties together to actually produce, improve, or customize a product or service.

While some scholars recognized the theory of co-creation in the late 20th century, it was really in the 21st century that the internet brought the concept to the forefront. Crowdsourcing has become a critical tool for engagement. Waze — apps like Waze, the navigation app used by millions of people is a good example of this concept. The widespread application of digital technology has made customer empowerment a must.

So society is rapidly moving from a passive to a more participatory consumer culture. Co-creation plays a key role in this, since today’s consumers want a say in creating new products and services or improving existing ones. In an article in the early 2000s, the Harvard Business Review talks about “Co-opting Customer Competence,” and how customers are transitioning from being a passive audience to being a more active player-becoming “co-creators as well as consumers of value.” And in this age of co-creation… and I think BNI was one of the leading organizations on co-creation, because in 1986 I formed the Board of Advisors and the Board of Advisors, made up of [BNI] members, created every single policy that exists in this organization. They either created the policy or approved the policies that were in existence, and they created many of them. They altered many of them. And so BNI has, from its early days, been the leader in co-creation.

And I believe that, in the age of co-creation, consumers want to work together with their favorite brands to ensure that products and services are adapted to meet their needs. In addition, many want to make sure that there’s a social cause related to the brand. And for those of you who don’t know, BNI, we have a foundation, we try to talk about the Foundation as often as possible. The BNI Foundation supports children in education. If you do a search on BNI Podcast, in quotation marks “BNI Foundation,” you’ll find many podcasts that have been done over the years, including several with my late wife, who was the co-founder of the BNI Foundation with me. And BNI has a social cause, and our social cause is children and education through the BNI Foundation.

So organizations engage in co-creation because they wish to foster the buy-in of stakeholder interest and increase the value through innovation. And Priscilla, that’s what this book is all about. It’s about the 3rd paradigm or co-creation. And as I mentioned in a previous episode where I talked about the book, episode 835, we surveyed 4000 people, and the book really talks at length about what works with co-creation, what doesn’t work with co-creation. And you don’t have to be a big company to employ the concepts of co-creation You can be a small company and start to use the power of co-creation into the 21st century.

And so I invite all my listeners to take a look at my book, “The 3rd Paradigm,” it’s on Amazon and in bookstores. And if you get a copy, would you please leave a review? Reviews really do help promote books and this book certainly mentions BNI multiple times and it’s great PR for the organization. That’s my message for today, Priscilla.

Priscilla: 
Great. It’s so fascinating. Thank you, Ivan. Are we done? Do you have more to add?

Ivan: (chuckling)
If you have any questions, I have lots more to add, but that’s everything I’ve got for today.

Priscilla:
Okay, great.

This podcast is sponsored by www.misneraudioprograms.com. These audio programs will provide you with the tools and the inspiration to powerfully enhance your BNI experience. So check out the great material available to you at www.misneraudioprograms.com and then use the promo code IVAN50 for 50% off of everything [this code is good for a limited time]. All of the proceeds go to the BNI Foundation.

Thank you so much for listening. This is Priscilla Rice, and we look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

Comments

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Sally M. Chetwynd says

    January 17, 2024 at 1:53 pm

    A civil engineering firm I worked for in Virginia, from 1996-2002, was involved in this 3rd paradigm idea. We used computer-aided drafting (CAD) software from a dominant firm in the industry, and we had an agreement with that firm to communicate with them regularly about what aspects of new versions of the drafting platform worked well and what didn’t. The firm couldn’t test the software in the field themselves; they gave us a discount for providing that field-testing.

    Reply

  2. Yoshihisa Sugiyama says

    January 17, 2024 at 5:08 pm

    In the 3rd Paradigm era, it is important the environment of the place that accepts other’s opinions, ideas, and methods and also we are in which place by my choice with whom.

    We have just moved to live in the 3rd Paradigm era however I imagine which kinds of Pradaigm coming to the 4th Paradigm era.

    Yoshihisa Sugiyama
    BNI JAPAN Rising-J

    Reply

  3. Ruth Padilla says

    January 22, 2024 at 2:24 pm

    Thank you,

    I would to thank you for the information that its giving me on this chapter. Is always helps me to understand better BNI

    Reply

    Gracias por el Podcast

    Co- creation is something that I have noticed a lot through sharing feedback. Listening to client feedback and improve accordingly has become a driver of success.
    Thank you for explaining this in a simple way.
